title: Training for the Uphill Athlete: A Manual for Mountain Runners and Ski Mountaineers
excerpt: The definitive training manual for mountain endurance athletes, translating exercise physiology into practical protocols for vertical running, ski mountaineering, and alpinism. Features the MAF heart rate method and polarized training framework.
rationale: Root book #3 for Exercise Science & Physical Training. Provides the sport-specific training manual for mountain athletes, complementing Born to Run's inspiration and Endure's neuroscience with a practical, evidence-based training system.
keyIdeas: Aerobic efficiency training (Zone 1-2) is the foundation of mountain performance, not high-intensity intervals
The MAF heart rate method is the most effective approach for building the aerobic base needed for uphill performance
Periodization and specificity are critical—mountain athletes need different training from road runners
Strength training for mountain athletes should focus on eccentric loading and endurance, not maximal power
Recovery and nutrition are training tools, not afterthoughts
keyTakeaways: Build a massive aerobic base through months of low-heart-rate training before adding intensity
Use heart rate drift (decoupling) to monitor aerobic fitness improvements and detect overtraining
Practice uphill and downhill technique separately—downhill eccentric loading is a unique training stimulus
Periodize training into base, build, and specific preparation phases aligned with goals
Caloric intake during mountain efforts must be higher than most athletes assume
whoShouldRead: Mountain runners, trail runners, and ultrarunners seeking structured training guidance
Ski mountaineers and alpinists looking for evidence-based preparation protocols
Coaches working with mountain sport athletes
Any endurance athlete interested in aerobic base training principles
whoShouldSkip: Road runners focused on flat speed
Gym-based strength athletes with no mountain or vertical component to their training
Readers seeking a general fitness book rather than a sport-specific manual
Athletes who prefer intuitive training over structured programs
